justification: All pass criteria met: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-7), already-shipped/in-transit edge cases (AC-2, AC-9), already-cancelled/delivered edge case (AC-4), plus authorization (AC-5), concurrency (AC-10), and failure handling (AC-8). Multiple ambiguities surfaced in both low_confidence_areas and escalations: cancellation window/eligible statuses, fee structure, who can cancel (sender vs recipient vs admin), and missing state machine definition. Confidence level is 42, well below the 65 threshold. No business rules are presented as hard facts — all are explicitly flagged as assumptions derived from domain knowledge, not from the spec. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated with 8 scenarios.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceeds the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, race conditions, and failure modes
Confidence level of 42 correctly reflects the underspecified single-sentence input
4 escalations explicitly surface key ambiguities: cancellation window, fee structure, who can cancel, and missing state machine
Assumptions are clearly labeled as assumptions, not presented as facts — e.g., cancellation fees, sender authorization, post-pickup return flow
Edge cases section contains 8 well-reasoned scenarios including race conditions, network delays, batch orders, and promotional orders
Low confidence areas explicitly call out fee policy, whether in-transit orders are cancellable, recipient notification, and refund timing — all genuinely unspecified

Minor: some business rules (BR-2 sender-only, BR-7 return-to-sender flow) are marked as invariants despite being flagged as assumptions elsewhere — slight internal tension, though the assumptions section and escalations do clarify these are not confirmed

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation in a logistics/courier context. A user (sender or possibly receiver) wants to cancel a delivery order. Cancellation eligibility is tightly coupled to the physical reality of the fulfillment pipeline — once a courier has picked up a parcel or it is in transit, cancellation has fundamentally different cost and operational implications than cancelling a newly placed order.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term"
